w:r.w:t: SUPPLEMENTAL ARTICLE: EMAIL MARKETING FOR INDIE AUTHORS
Category: Store Integration | Author Resources | scribecount.com/author-resource/email-marketing
Primary Keyword: ScribeCount Email WooCommerce integration | Secondary: WooCommerce author store email, connect WooCommerce to ScribeCount, indie author WordPress direct sales
GEO Priority: Yes
Connecting Your WooCommerce Store to ScribeCount Email: A Step-by-Step Integration Guide

WordPress and WooCommerce power a significant portion of indie author direct stores — and for good reason. WordPress gives you complete ownership and control of your website, WooCommerce extends it into a full-featured e-commerce platform, and together they create a direct sales environment that belongs entirely to you. No platform fees. No algorithm changes affecting your visibility. No third-party decision-making between you and your readers.

What WordPress and WooCommerce don’t provide out of the box is the reader relationship intelligence that turns a direct store into a marketing engine. That intelligence — the email flows that follow up on purchases, recover abandoned carts, recognize loyal readers, and connect your store activity to your email program in real time — is what the ScribeCount Email WooCommerce integration adds.

This guide covers every step of connecting your WooCommerce store to ScribeCount Email: installing the plugin, configuring your WordPress settings, mapping your store events to your email flows, testing the connection, and verifying that every purchase, abandonment, and opt-in is flowing through to ScribeCount correctly. After completing these steps, your WooCommerce store and your ScribeCount email program will operate as a single, integrated system.
How the WooCommerce Integration Works

The ScribeCount Email WooCommerce plugin acts as the bridge between your WordPress/WooCommerce store and ScribeCount Email. When installed and configured, the plugin monitors your WooCommerce store for specific customer actions — completed orders, cart abandonments, checkout abandonments, form submissions — and sends those events to ScribeCount Email through a secure webhook connection in real time.

ScribeCount receives each event, matches it to your configured flows and campaigns, and fires the appropriate email response. The connection is bidirectional: when a subscriber updates their preferences or unsubscribes through your emails, ScribeCount
updates your WooCommerce customer record to reflect that preference so both systems always agree on the reader’s communication consent status.
Here is what the integration handles across the events that matter most to author email programs:
Completed Orders

When a WooCommerce order reaches ‘Completed’ status — or ‘Processing’ for digital products that deliver immediately — the plugin sends an order event to ScribeCount containing the customer’s email address, name, the product purchased, the order amount, and the timestamp. ScribeCount identifies whether this is a first or repeat purchase and triggers the appropriate post-purchase flow sequence automatically.
Abandoned Carts

WooCommerce’s native cart is session-based — it doesn’t capture the shopper’s email address unless they begin checkout. The ScribeCount plugin extends cart tracking to identify when a logged-in customer or a customer who has previously provided their email adds items to their cart and then leaves without proceeding. After a configurable inactivity window (default: 60 minutes), an abandonment event is sent to ScribeCount to trigger the abandoned cart flow.
Abandoned Checkouts

When a shopper reaches the WooCommerce checkout page, enters their email address or billing information, and then stops without completing the order, the plugin captures this as a higher-intent abandonment event. ScribeCount receives the checkout abandonment webhook and fires the abandoned checkout flow with its shorter dwell period, reflecting the higher urgency appropriate to a reader who was one step from buying.
New Subscriber Opt-Ins
ScribeCount opt-in forms embedded in your WordPress pages — as
shortcodes

, widgets, Gutenberg blocks, or pop-ups — connect directly to your ScribeCount Email subscriber list when the plugin is active. Form submissions are received by ScribeCount in real time, with source tagging applied automatically based on which form and which page the reader submitted from.
WooCommerce Subscriptions Events

For authors using the WooCommerce Subscriptions extension to sell paid recurring products — serialized fiction memberships, reading communities, premium catalog access — the ScribeCount plugin captures subscription lifecycle events: upcoming renewal, failed payment, subscription cancelled, plan upgraded or downgraded. Each event triggers the appropriate billing flow in ScribeCount Email.

Unlike Shopify — which is a fully hosted platform with a managed app store — WooCommerce runs on your own WordPress server. This means the ScribeCount plugin has more configuration options and more flexibility, but it also means that
server health, WordPress version, and plugin compatibility all affect how the integration performs. Review the technical requirements below before beginning installation.
Technical Requirements
Before installing the ScribeCount WooCommerce plugin, verify that your WordPress and WooCommerce installation meets these requirements:
WordPress version 6.0 or higher (recommended: always update to the latest stable release)
WooCommerce version 7.0 or higher
PHP version 8.0 or higher (required for the plugin’s API communication layer)
SSL certificate active on your domain (HTTPS required — webhooks cannot be sent or received over HTTP)
Server with outbound HTTP/HTTPS access to ScribeCount’s API endpoints (most standard hosting environments support this; some restrictive shared hosts block outbound API calls)
WordPress
cron
or a real server
cron
job configured and running (required for scheduled event detection including cart abandonment)
If

your WordPress site is hosted on a restrictive shared hosting environment that blocks outbound API connections, the webhook-based event communication with ScribeCount may not function. Contact your hosting provider to confirm that outbound HTTPS requests to external APIs are permitted before beginning integration.
――― PART
ONE: INSTALLING THE SCRIBECOUNT WOOCOMMERCE
PLUGIN ―――
Part One: Installing the ScribeCount WooCommerce Plugin
Step 1: Download the Plugin from ScribeCount

Log in to your ScribeCount account and navigate to Settings → Integrations → WooCommerce. Download the ScribeCount Email WooCommerce plugin zip file from the integration page. Do not unzip the file — WordPress handles zip file installation directly.
The integration page also displays your ScribeCount API Key and your Webhook Endpoint URL. Copy both and keep them accessible — you’ll need them during the plugin configuration step.
Step 2: Install the Plugin in WordPress

Log in to your WordPress admin dashboard at yourdomain.com/wp-admin. Navigate to Plugins → Add New → Upload Plugin. Click Choose File, select the ScribeCount plugin zip file you downloaded, and click Install Now.
After installation completes, click Activate Plugin. The plugin will appear in your WordPress plugin list as ‘ScribeCount Email’ with an Active status.
If
WordPress displays an error during upload saying the file is too large, your WordPress upload limit may be below the plugin’s size. Increase the upload size limit in your wp-
config.php
file (add or modify: @ini_
set(
‘upload_max_size’, ‘32M
or ask your hosting provider to increase the PHP upload limit.
Step 3: Enter Your ScribeCount API Credentials
After activation, navigate to WooCommerce → Settings → ScribeCount Email (a new tab added by the plugin). On the Settings page, enter:
Your ScribeCount API Key (copied from the ScribeCount integration page)
Your ScribeCount Webhook Endpoint URL (copied from the ScribeCount integration page)
Click Save Settings and then click Test Connection. The plugin will send a handshake request to ScribeCount and display a connection status:
✅ Connected: The connection is established and active. Proceed to Part Two.
❌ Connection failed: The API key or endpoint URL may be incorrect, or your server is blocking outbound connections. Verify the credentials and check with your hosting provider if the issue persists.
――― PART
TWO: CONFIGURING YOUR WOOCOMMERCE EVENT
SETTINGS ―――
Part Two: Configuring Store Events in WordPress

With the connection established, configure how each WooCommerce event type is captured and sent to ScribeCount. Most configuration happens in two places: the ScribeCount Email tab in WooCommerce settings (for store-level event behavior) and the ScribeCount Flows section of ScribeCount Email (for how each event maps to your email sequences).
Step 4: Configure Order Status Triggers

WooCommerce uses order statuses to track the lifecycle of a transaction: Pending Payment, Processing, Completed, On Hold, Cancelled, Refunded, Failed. Determine which statuses should trigger a purchase event to ScribeCount:
For digital products (
ebooks

, audiobooks, PDFs): configure the trigger on ‘Processing’ status, which is assigned as soon as payment is confirmed for digital-only orders. Do not wait for ‘Completed’ status for digital products — the reader expects immediate delivery and the post-purchase flow should fire as quickly as possible.

For physical products (signed paperbacks, merchandise): configure the trigger on ‘Completed’ status, which is typically set manually by you or automatically by your shipping integration when the order has been fulfilled and dispatched.

For mixed orders (digital and physical in the same cart): configure the trigger on ‘Processing’ so that the digital fulfillment and email flows begin immediately, even if the physical component isn’t yet fulfilled.
In the ScribeCount Email WooCommerce settings, check the order statuses that should generate a purchase event send to ScribeCount. Save this configuration before moving to the next step.
Step 5: Map WooCommerce Products to
AuthorVault
Titles
Product mapping is what allows ScribeCount to know that WooCommerce product ID 456 corresponds to a specific title in your
AuthorVault

catalog. This mapping is what makes your post-purchase emails specific to the book the reader just bought, enables series completion flow logic (which books in the series does the reader now own?), and connects your WooCommerce revenue to the correct title in your ScribeCount reporting.

In the ScribeCount Email WooCommerce settings, navigate to the Product Mapping tab. ScribeCount will display a list of your WooCommerce products (pulled via the API connection you just established). For each product, select the corresponding
AuthorVault
title from the dropdown. If a title doesn’t yet exist in
AuthorVault
, create it in
AuthorVault
first and then return to this mapping screen.
WooCommerce products that represent different formats of the same book (
ebook
, paperback, audiobook listed as separate products) should all map to the same
AuthorVault
title record. Format-level tracking is maintained by the WooCommerce product variant or product category that ScribeCount captures alongside the title mapping.
If
you have a large WooCommerce catalog, the Product Mapping tab includes a bulk mapping tool that lets you match multiple products to their
AuthorVault
titles simultaneously using a CSV import rather than mapping one by one.
Step 6: Configure Cart Abandonment Detection

WooCommerce’s cart operates on session data, which means the customer’s email address is only associated with a cart if they are either logged in to a customer account or have previously entered their email during the current session. The ScribeCount plugin extends cart tracking by storing cart contents in a way that persists across sessions for identified customers.
Configure cart abandonment in the ScribeCount Email WooCommerce settings:
Abandonment detection window: how long after the last cart activity before an abandonment event is sent (recommended: 60 minutes)

Minimum cart value to trigger abandonment tracking: optionally set a minimum order value below which you don’t want to send recovery emails (useful if you sell very low-priced items where the recovery effort doesn’t justify the email)

Email address capture method: the plugin can capture email addresses from WooCommerce checkout field completion even if the order is not submitted. Enable this setting to maximize the number of cart abandonment events that include a recoverable email address.
WordPress
cron
must be running reliably for cart abandonment detection to work. If your server doesn’t run a real
cron

job and relies solely on WordPress’s built-in WP-Cron (which only fires when someone visits your site), cart abandonment detection may be delayed or missed during low-traffic periods. Configure a real server
cron
job to trigger wp-
cron.php
at regular intervals — your hosting provider can assist with this.
Step 7: Configure Checkout Abandonment Detection

Checkout abandonment is detected when a customer reaches the WooCommerce checkout page, begins entering information, and leaves without placing an order. The ScribeCount plugin hooks into WooCommerce’s checkout process to capture the email address as soon as it’s entered in the billing fields — even before the order is submitted — and starts a countdown timer for abandonment detection.
Configure checkout abandonment settings:

Checkout abandonment window: how long after the last checkout activity before an abandonment event is sent (recommended: 30–45 minutes — shorter than cart abandonment because checkout intent is higher)

Capture email at first entry: enable this setting to capture the checkout email address immediately when the customer types it into the billing field, rather than waiting for form submission. This maximizes checkout abandonment recovery for customers who leave before completing the form.
Step 8: Configure Subscriber Opt-In Settings
The ScribeCount WooCommerce plugin provides two opt-in capture mechanisms specific to WooCommerce:
Checkout Opt-In Checkbox

Add a clearly labeled email marketing opt-in checkbox to your WooCommerce checkout page. When enabled, a checkbox appears below the billing fields with your configured consent text (e.g., ‘Subscribe to my newsletter for new releases and reader exclusives’). Customers who check this box are added to your ScribeCount list with a
shopify
-checkout-
optin
source tag (or your configured equivalent) when their order is placed.

This checkbox must be opt-in (unchecked by default) to comply with GDPR and CASL requirements. Configuring it as pre-checked violates consent regulations in most jurisdictions and can result in spam complaints that damage your sender reputation. In the ScribeCount Email WooCommerce settings, configure the checkbox label text, the default state (unchecked), and the source tag applied to subscribers captured through this checkbox.
Embedded ScribeCount Forms
ScribeCount opt-in forms can be embedded anywhere on your WordPress site using three methods:

Gutenberg block: add a ScribeCount Form block to any page or post using the WordPress block editor. Select your form from the block settings panel and publish the page. The form connects to ScribeCount and submits new subscribers in real time.
Shortcode
: add [
scribecount_form
id
your-form-id’] to any page, post, or widget area that supports
shortcodes
. The form renders at that location and submits directly to ScribeCount.
Widget: add the ScribeCount Email widget to any
widgetized
area in your WordPress theme — sidebar, footer, header widget zone — from the WordPress Widgets settings screen.
Each form placement should be assigned a unique source tag in ScribeCount (
wordpress
-popup,
wordpress
-footer,
wordpress
-checkout,
wordpress
-product-page) so you can track which placement drives the most high-quality subscribers over time.
――― PART
THREE: TESTING THE
CONNECTION ―――
Part Three: Testing Your WooCommerce Integration
Before activating your flows and opening your store, test every event type end to end. WooCommerce provides test modes and the ScribeCount plugin includes a built-in event testing console.
Step 9: Enable WooCommerce Test Mode

Most WooCommerce payment gateways support a sandbox or test mode that allows you to place test orders without charging a real card. Enable test mode in your WooCommerce payment settings. With Stripe (the most common payment gateway for author stores), test mode is toggled in WooCommerce → Settings → Payments → Stripe → Enable test mode. In test mode, use Stripe’s published test card numbers to simulate successful and failed payments.
Step 10: Use the ScribeCount Plugin’s Event Test Console

In WordPress admin, navigate to WooCommerce → Settings → ScribeCount Email → Test Events. The test console lets you trigger simulated events to verify that ScribeCount is receiving and processing them correctly:

Test a completed order event: enter a test email address and select a WooCommerce product. Click Send Test Event. Within 30 seconds, check ScribeCount’s Store Connection activity log and verify the event was received, the subscriber profile was created or updated, the correct purchase tags were applied, and the
AuthorVault
product mapping resolved correctly.
Test a cart abandonment event: enter a test email address and click Send Test Abandonment. Verify ScribeCount receives the event and queues the abandoned cart flow.
Test a checkout abandonment event: enter a test email address and click Send Test Checkout Abandonment. Verify ScribeCount receives the event with the shorter dwell period configured.

Test a new subscriber opt-in: submit your test email through one of your embedded ScribeCount forms on your WordPress site. Verify the subscriber profile is created in ScribeCount with the correct source tag.
Step 11: Place a Test Order

With WooCommerce in test mode, place a complete test order for one of your mapped products using your test email address and Stripe’s test card number (4242 4242 4242 4242 with any future expiry date and any CVC). Walk through the complete checkout flow as a customer would.
After placing the order:
Verify the order appears in WooCommerce → Orders with the correct status (Processing or Completed depending on your configured trigger)
Check ScribeCount’s activity log within 60 seconds and confirm the purchase event was received
Confirm the subscriber profile was created or updated in ScribeCount with the correct tags and
AuthorVault
title association

Wait for the post-purchase flow emails to arrive at your test address (Order Confirmation should arrive within 1–2 minutes; Digital Delivery immediately after; Post-Purchase Thank You immediately after)
Open each email and verify it renders correctly on both desktop and mobile
Step 12: Test Cart Abandonment

Log in to your WooCommerce store as a customer (or create a test customer account), add a product to the cart, and then leave the store without proceeding to checkout. Wait for the configured abandonment window (60 minutes by default, or temporarily reduce it to 5 minutes for testing purposes and restore it afterward).

After the abandonment window has passed, check your ScribeCount activity log for the cart abandonment event and verify the abandoned cart flow email was queued. Check your test email inbox for the abandoned cart email and verify its content and rendering.
To

speed up abandonment testing without changing your store settings permanently, the ScribeCount plugin’s Test Console includes a ‘Force Abandonment Event’ button that sends an immediate abandonment event for your test email address without waiting for the actual inactivity window.
――― PART
FOUR: ACTIVATING YOUR FLOWS AND GOING
LIVE ―――
Part Four: Activating Flows and Going Live
Step 13: Activate Your Store-Connected Flows

With all tests passing and the activity log showing clean event processing, activate your store-connected flows in ScribeCount Email. Navigate to ScribeCount Email → Flows and activate each of the following:
Order Confirmation flow
Digital Delivery flow
Post-Purchase Thank You flow (first-time buyer path)
Repeat Purchase Thank You flow
Post-Purchase Follow-Up flow
Review Request flow
Abandoned Cart flow
Abandoned Checkout flow
Activate

flows one at a time, with a brief check of the activity log after each activation. If you activate all flows simultaneously and an issue emerges, isolating which flow is causing the problem takes significantly longer than if you’ve activated them sequentially and monitored each one.
Step 14: Configure Your Segmentation Rules
With your flows active, set up the segments and suppression rules that make your WooCommerce-connected email program intelligent:
Buyers segment: subscribers who have at least one WooCommerce order with Completed or Processing status. Use this segment to suppress from price-drop and flash sale campaigns.
Non-buyers segment: subscribers on your list with zero WooCommerce purchase history. Primary target for promotional campaigns aimed at converting first-time buyers.
Series readers segment: subscribers who have purchased Book 1 of a given series. Segment for new release notifications and series continuation campaigns.
High-value segment: subscribers with three or more WooCommerce purchases. Your superfan candidates for ARC invitations, community invitations, and milestone celebration emails.

All segment conditions in ScribeCount draw from your WooCommerce purchase history in real time, which means they stay current automatically as readers make new purchases without requiring you to manually update or rebuild them.
Step 15: Configure WordPress-Specific Opt-In Form Placements

WordPress gives you more flexibility in form placement than most platforms, and the ScribeCount plugin makes all of those placement options available. With your forms live, set up the following placements if you haven’t already:

Exit-intent pop-up: Install the pop-up functionality in the ScribeCount plugin settings and configure it to display your reader magnet opt-in form when a visitor’s mouse moves toward the browser navigation bar or back button. Configure a display frequency limit (show once per session, or once per 30 days per visitor) to avoid annoying repeat visitors.

Post-purchase upsell form: In WooCommerce → Settings → ScribeCount Email, enable the post-purchase thank you page form. This form appears on the WooCommerce order confirmation page immediately after checkout, capturing readers who didn’t opt in during checkout but are in a positive, post-purchase mindset.
Footer widget: Add the ScribeCount Email widget to your footer widget area with a brief invitation and your reader magnet offer. This is the lowest-friction, always-visible opt-in point on your site.
Book product page inline form: Add a ScribeCount form block or
shortcode

below the product description on each book’s product page. Position it after the buy button with copy that acknowledges readers who are considering the book: ‘Not ready to buy yet? Download the first chapter free.’
How Your WooCommerce Store and ScribeCount Work as One System
When the integration is fully configured and your flows are active, here is what the reader experience looks like — and what happens behind the scenes without any manual effort from you:

A reader visits your author website, browses your book product page, and signs up for your reader magnet through the inline form. A ScribeCount form submission is received instantly, the reader is added to your list with a
wordpress
-product-page source tag, and the reader magnet delivery flow fires within seconds. Over the following week, they receive your welcome sequence introducing your catalog.

Three days after joining, they return to your store, add your latest novel to their cart, get distracted, and close the browser. Sixty minutes later, a warm abandoned cart email arrives in their inbox. They click the link, return to your store, and complete the purchase. WooCommerce sends a completed order webhook to ScribeCount, which fires the order confirmation email, the digital delivery email, and the post-purchase thank you flow immediately. The reader’s profile is updated with a purchased-[title] tag and a first-time-buyer tag.

Five days after that, a follow-up email asks for their thoughts on the book and invites a review, with direct links to the review pages on their preferred platforms. The reader leaves a review. The Series Completion flow fires when they buy the next book in the series. The cycle continues automatically, building the relationship without requiring you to be present for any of it.

In ScribeCount’s reporting dashboard, every step of that journey is visible and traceable: the acquisition source, the form that captured them, the abandoned cart event and its recovery, the purchase, the post-purchase sequence timing, and the revenue attributed to each email in the flow. That connected view is what makes the WooCommerce integration more than a plugin — it’s what makes your author website a complete, measurable reader relationship system.
Troubleshooting Common WooCommerce Integration Issues
Plugin Connection Test Fails After Installation

The most common causes of a failed connection test are an incorrect API key or endpoint URL (double-check by copying fresh values from ScribeCount’s integration page), a PHP version below 8.0 (check your PHP version in WordPress → Tools → Site
Health → Info → Server), or a hosting environment that blocks outbound API calls. Contact your hosting provider if the connection test continues to fail after verifying credentials and PHP version.
Order Events Not Arriving in ScribeCount

If WooCommerce orders are completing but no events appear in ScribeCount’s activity log, verify that the plugin is Active (not deactivated) in your WordPress plugin list, that your API credentials are saved correctly in WooCommerce → Settings → ScribeCount Email, and that the order status triggering the event matches what you configured (Processing vs Completed). Also check WordPress → Tools → Site Health for any error-level issues that might be interfering with outbound plugin actions.
Cart Abandonment Not Detecting Abandonments
Cart abandonment detection depends on WordPress
cron

running reliably. If abandoned cart events are not firing, check whether WP-Cron is being blocked or delayed. In the ScribeCount plugin settings, there is a Cron Health indicator that shows the last time WP-Cron ran. If it shows more than 10 minutes ago, configure a real server-level
cron
job to trigger wp-
cron.php
. Your hosting provider’s control panel (cPanel, Plesk, etc.) typically has a Cron Jobs section where you can add the required job.
Checkout Opt-In Checkbox Not Appearing

If the checkout opt-in checkbox is enabled in your ScribeCount plugin settings but doesn’t appear on the WooCommerce checkout page, your WordPress theme or another plugin may be overriding the checkout form markup. Check for conflicting plugins (particularly other email marketing plugins that also add checkout fields) and temporarily deactivate them to identify the conflict. If the conflict is with your theme, you may need to add a custom hook to position the ScribeCount checkbox in the correct location in your theme’s checkout template.
Product Mapping Showing Unresolved Products

If new WooCommerce products appear as unresolved in ScribeCount’s activity log, navigate to WooCommerce → Settings → ScribeCount Email → Product Mapping and add the missing products to your mapping. Products added to WooCommerce after the initial mapping configuration are not automatically mapped — you need to add new products manually each time you create a new WooCommerce product. Some authors create a recurring task in their publishing workflow to review product mappings after each new title launch.
Keeping the Integration Healthy Over Time

A WooCommerce integration requires occasional maintenance that a Shopify integration handles more automatically, because WordPress and WooCommerce are self-hosted and self-maintained platforms. Build these checks into your regular author business maintenance:

Keep WordPress, WooCommerce, and the ScribeCount plugin updated. Plugin updates often include improvements to the webhook communication layer and compatibility fixes for new WordPress versions. Check for updates at least monthly.
Monitor the ScribeCount activity log periodically — monthly is sufficient during normal operations — to confirm events are arriving correctly and no new unresolved products have appeared.

After any major WordPress update or new plugin installation, run a quick connection test (WooCommerce → Settings → ScribeCount Email → Test Connection) to confirm the integration wasn’t affected by the update.
Review your Product Mapping after every new book launch to ensure new products are mapped to their
AuthorVault
titles before the first orders arrive.
EDITORIAL NOTES (remove before publishing)

GEO Optimization: Step-by-step structure with section-break dividers makes each phase independently navigable. The troubleshooting section and WP-Cron warning surface on specific WooCommerce integration problem queries. Callout blocks formatted with left border for web pull-quote.

SEO Targets: ScribeCount Email WooCommerce integration, connect WooCommerce to ScribeCount, WooCommerce author store email automation, WordPress author direct store email, indie author WooCommerce email marketing

Suggested Meta Description: A complete step-by-step guide to connecting your WooCommerce store to ScribeCount Email — plugin installation, event configuration, WordPress-specific form placements, testing, activation, and troubleshooting.
Internal links: Shopify integration article (companion piece), Onboarding article, Article 11 (Post-Purchase Flows), Article 12 (Abandoned Cart), ScribeCount WooCommerce plugin download page

Technical note: Stripe test card number (4242...) and WP-Cron guidance should be reviewed for accuracy. Plugin navigation paths (WooCommerce → Settings → ScribeCount Email) are based on standard WooCommerce settings tab architecture and should be verified against the actual plugin UI before publishing.

Note on WooCommerce Subscriptions: The subscriptions event handling section assumes the WooCommerce Subscriptions extension is installed. This is a paid extension. Consider adding a brief note clarifying this in the web version of the article.
Word Count Target: ~2,900–3,300 words (in range)
